algos.name: Two Pointers
algos.desc: Uses two indices moving toward each other or in the same direction to solve problems in O(n) instead of O(n²). Ideal for sorted arrays and substring problems.
approach: Sort the array if needed
Initialize left = 0, right = n-1 (or both at start for same-direction)
Move pointers based on a condition (sum, window, match)
Continue until pointers meet or cross
algos.tip: Use two pointers whenever you see: sorted array + pair/triplet sum, palindrome check, or container-with-most-water style problems.

algos.name: Sliding Window
algos.desc: Maintains a window of elements that satisfies a condition. Window expands (right pointer) and shrinks (left pointer) to find optimal subarray/substring.
approach: Initialize left = 0, right = 0, window state (map/set/count)
Expand window by moving right, update state
When window violates condition, shrink by moving left
Track answer (max/min window size, or count) at each valid state
algos.tip: Fixed-window: slide directly. Variable-window: use HashMap for char frequency. Trigger: 'longest/shortest subarray with condition'.

questions.name: Max Sum Subarray of size K

algos.name: Prefix Sum
algos.desc: Precomputes cumulative sums so range sum queries are answered in O(1). Foundation for many subarray problems.
approach: Build prefix array: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]
Range sum [l, r] = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]
For subarray sum = k, use HashMap: count of (prefix[i] - k)
algos.tip: Always initialize map with {0:1} to handle subarrays starting from index 0.

algos.name: Array Operations & Linear Search
algos.desc: An Array is a contiguous block of memory. Linear Search traverses elements one by one until the target is found.
approach: Iterate over indices from 0 to N-1.
Compare elements with the search target.
Return the element or its index if found.
algos.tip: Fundamental fallback when elements are entirely unsorted or when you must guarantee exhaustive evaluation.

algos.name: Merge Sort
algos.desc: Divide-and-conquer algorithm. Splits array into halves, recursively sorts, then merges. Stable sort, guaranteed O(n log n).
approach: Base case: array of size ≤ 1 is sorted
Find mid = (low + high) / 2
Recursively sort left half and right half
Merge two sorted halves into auxiliary array
Copy back to original
algos.tip: Preferred when stability matters or for external sorting. Use Arrays.sort() for primitives (quicksort internally) and Collections.sort() for objects (TimSort = merge sort variant).

algos.name: Quick Sort
algos.desc: Picks a pivot, partitions array into elements smaller/larger than pivot, then recursively sorts. In-place with O(log n) average stack space.
approach: Choose pivot (last element, random, or median-of-3)
Partition: rearrange so all < pivot are left, all > pivot are right
Recursively apply to left and right partitions
Randomize pivot to avoid O(n²) worst case
algos.tip: Use randomized pivot to avoid O(n²) on sorted input. Dutch flag partition handles duplicates efficiently.

algos.name: Heap Sort
algos.desc: Builds a max-heap from the array, then repeatedly extracts the maximum element. In-place, O(n log n), not stable.
approach: Build max-heap by calling heapify from n/2-1 down to 0
Swap root (max) with last element, reduce heap size
Call heapify on root
Repeat until heap size is 1
algos.tip: Guaranteed O(n log n) and O(1) space — best of both worlds, but poor cache performance makes it slower than quicksort in practice.

algos.name: Bubble Sort
algos.desc: A simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order.
approach: Loop over the array elements.
In an inner loop, compare adjacent elements.
Swap if the left element is greater than the right element.
Optimize by keeping a boolean flag to track if a swap occurred.
algos.tip: Rarely used in practice due to poor performance, but often asked as a conceptual or warm-up question.

algos.name: Selection Sort
algos.desc: An in-place comparison sort that divides the input list into two parts: a sorted sublist and an unsorted sublist. It repeatedly selects the minimum element from the unsorted sublist.
approach: Iterate through the array.
For each position, find the index of the minimum element in the remaining unsorted portion.
Swap the minimum element with the current position.
algos.tip: Maintains minimal number of swaps (O(n) swaps overall), which can be useful when memory write operations are heavily penalized.

algos.name: Insertion Sort
algos.desc: Builds the final sorted array one item at a time. It is much less efficient on large lists than more advanced algorithms such as quicksort, heapsort, or merge sort.
approach: Assume the first element is already sorted.
Pick the next element.
Compare with all elements in the sorted sub-list and shift them to the right to create space.
Insert the value.
algos.tip: Highly efficient for very small datasets or naturally mostly-sorted arrays (O(n) time).

algos.name: Counting / Radix / Bucket Sort
algos.desc: Non-comparison-based sorting algorithms used to sort keys in specific ranges. Useful when the range of input values is relatively small or well-distributed.
approach: Counting Sort: Count occurrences of each value, then calculate running sum to determine sorted positions.
Radix Sort: Use Counting Sort digit by digit from least to most significant.
Bucket Sort: Distribute elements into buckets, sort each bucket, and concatenate.
algos.tip: If the problem mentions 'linear time sorting' and the values have a known upper bound, use Counting or Radix Sort.

algos.name: Classic Binary Search
algos.desc: Finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search space. Template: [lo, hi] closed interval.
approach: Set lo = 0, hi = n-1
While lo <= hi: mid = lo + (hi-lo)/2
If arr[mid] == target: return mid
If arr[mid] < target: lo = mid+1 else hi = mid-1
algos.tip: Always use mid = lo + (hi-lo)/2 to avoid integer overflow. For lower_bound use lo <= hi with hi = mid-1.

algos.name: Binary Search on Answer
algos.desc: When answer lies in a monotonic search space (not an array), binary search on the answer value itself. The feasibility function is monotonic.
approach: Identify the search space: [lo, hi] = [min_answer, max_answer]
Write a canAchieve(mid) boolean function
Binary search: if canAchieve(mid) → move right, else move left
Return lo at the end
algos.tip: Key insight: if a value X is feasible, all values > X are also feasible (or vice versa). Trigger words: 'minimize the maximum', 'maximize the minimum'.

algos.name: Fast & Slow Pointers (Floyd's)
algos.desc: Two pointers at different speeds to detect cycles, find middle, or find the nth node from end.
approach: Slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps
If cycle exists, fast and slow meet inside cycle
To find middle: when fast reaches end, slow is at middle
To find cycle start: after meeting, reset one to head, move both 1 step
algos.tip: Always check fast != null AND fast.next != null. For finding cycle entry: reset one pointer to head after collision.

algos.name: Reversal Techniques
algos.desc: Reverse entire list, sublist, or k-groups using three pointers: prev, curr, next.
approach: Initialize prev = null, curr = head
Save next = curr.next
Set curr.next = prev
Advance prev = curr, curr = next
For partial reversal, connect boundaries carefully
algos.tip: For reverse k-group: reverse, then recursively call on remaining. Draw before coding.

algos.name: Linked List Basics
algos.desc: A linear collection of data elements where order is not given by physical placement in memory. Each element points to the next.
approach: Maintain a pointer to the head.
Traverse sequentially using 'current = current.next'.
To modify links, carefully maintain previous node references.
algos.tip: Keep an eye on dummy head strategies to avoid painful edge cases when deleting or inserting at the head.

algos.name: Tree Traversals (DFS)
algos.desc: Preorder (root-left-right), Inorder (left-root-right), Postorder (left-right-root). Both recursive and iterative.
approach: Preorder: process node, recurse left, recurse right
Inorder: recurse left, process node, recurse right (BST → sorted order)
Postorder: recurse left, recurse right, process node
Iterative: use explicit stack; inorder uses stack + pointer pattern
algos.tip: Inorder of BST = sorted array. Postorder needed when child info is used by parent (e.g., height, diameter).

algos.name: BST Operations
algos.desc: Binary Search Tree: left < root < right. Supports search, insert, delete in O(log n) average.
approach: Search: compare with root → go left or right recursively
Insert: recurse to correct leaf position, insert node
Delete: three cases — no child, one child, two children (replace with inorder successor)
Validate BST: pass min/max bounds down recursively
algos.tip: For BST validation, pass (min, max) bounds — not just parent comparison. Inorder traverse to check if sorted.

algos.name: Segment Tree
algos.desc: A tree data structure used for storing intervals or segments, enabling fast querying of sums, mins, or maxes over ranges and fast point updates.
approach: The tree is an array where the root contains aggregated data for the entire array.
Left child covers the left half of the parent's segment, right child covers right half.
Leaves correspond to single elements.
algos.tip: Whenever you see arrays with mutable updates and range queries, use Segment Trees or Fenwick Trees.

algos.name: LCA & Tree DP
algos.desc: Techniques for finding the Lowest Common Ancestor or computing values bottom-up in a tree (Tree Dynamic Programming).
approach: For LCA: DFS downwards, return node if found. Ancestor is the node where both left and right return true.
For Tree DP: Compute states at leaf nodes and aggregate properties up to parent nodes.
algos.tip: Standard LCA recursive template is asked heavily in top tech interviews. Memorize it.

questions.name: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree

algos.name: Min/Max Heap Patterns
algos.desc: Java's PriorityQueue is a min-heap by default. Use for K-largest, K-smallest, merge K sorted, median maintenance.
approach: For K-largest: maintain min-heap of size K. If heap > K, poll
For K-smallest: maintain max-heap of size K
For median: use two heaps — maxHeap (lower half) + minHeap (upper half)
Poll top K elements for result
algos.tip: Two-heap pattern for median: maxHeap.size() = minHeap.size() or maxHeap.size() = minHeap.size()+1. Rebalance on each insert.

algos.name: Heap (Priority Queue) Basics
algos.desc: A specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property. In a Max Heap, the largest key is always at the top.
approach: Insertions and deletions maintain the heap property via 'bubble up' and 'bubble down'.
Allows O(1) access to min or max.
In Java, use PriorityQueue.
algos.tip: Extremely useful for 'Kth Largest/Smallest' problems, processing intervals scheduling, and Dijkstra's.

questions.name: Kth Largest Element in an Array

algos.name: Trie (Prefix Tree)
algos.desc: Tree where each node represents a character. Used for prefix search, autocomplete, and word dictionaries in O(L) time where L = word length.
approach: Node has children[26] (or HashMap) and isEnd boolean
Insert: traverse/create nodes for each character
Search: traverse nodes; check isEnd at last character
StartsWith: same as search but don't check isEnd
algos.tip: Use HashMap instead of array for non-lowercase or large alphabets. XOR Trie is used for maximum XOR subarray problems.

algos.name: HashMap / HashSet Fundamentals
algos.desc: A data structure that provides average O(1) time complexity for insertion, deletion, and lookup using a hash function.
approach: Initialize a Set to track uniqueness, or a Map to track key-value associations.
Use hashing to quickly map values to internal buckets.
Handles collisions via chaining (LinkedLists) or probing.
algos.tip: Use a HashSet to find duplicates or compute disjoint sets. Use a HashMap for frequency counting or 1:1 mapping lookups.

algos.name: Monotonic Stack
algos.desc: Stack that maintains elements in monotonically increasing or decreasing order. Used for next greater/smaller element, histograms, and span problems.
approach: Iterate through elements
While stack not empty and current violates monotonic property: pop and process
Push current element (or index)
Elements remaining in stack at end have no next greater/smaller
algos.tip: Push indices, not values — needed for range calculations. For circular array: iterate 2n with modulo.

algos.name: Stack Basics
algos.desc: A Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure. Useful for DFS iterative implementation, undo mechanisms, and parsing strings.
approach: Push elements to the top of the stack.
Pop elements from the top of the stack.
Peek to check the top without removing.
algos.tip: Always consider a Stack when evaluating valid paren configurations, decoding strings, or reversing items.

algos.name: Queue / Deque Basics
algos.desc: Queue: First-In-First-Out (FIFO). Deque: Double-Ended Queue (add/remove from both ends).
approach: Enqueue (offer) at the tail.
Dequeue (poll) from the head.
Useful for BFS (Level Order Traversals).
algos.tip: Use ArrayDeque over LinkedList in Java for Deque operations; it is generally faster and cache-friendly.

algos.name: BFS (Breadth-First Search)
algos.desc: Level-order traversal using a queue. Finds shortest path in unweighted graphs. Explores all neighbors before going deeper.
approach: Add source to queue, mark visited
While queue not empty: dequeue node, process it
Enqueue all unvisited neighbors, mark them visited
Track level/distance for shortest path
algos.tip: BFS = shortest path in unweighted graph. For multi-source BFS, add all sources at once. 0-1 BFS uses deque.

algos.name: DFS (Depth-First Search)
algos.desc: Explores as deep as possible before backtracking. Used for cycle detection, topological sort, connected components, path finding.
approach: Mark node visited
Recurse on all unvisited neighbors
For cycle detection: track recursion stack (white-gray-black)
Postorder DFS gives reverse topological order
algos.tip: For cycle detection in directed graph, maintain a 'in-stack' array. Topological sort only possible on DAGs.

algos.name: Dijkstra's Algorithm
algos.desc: Single-source shortest path in weighted graph with non-negative edges. Uses a min-heap (priority queue).
approach: Initialize dist[] = ∞, dist[src] = 0
Add (0, src) to min-heap
Pop min-cost node, skip if stale
Relax all neighbors: if dist[u]+w < dist[v], update and push
algos.tip: Fails on negative edges — use Bellman-Ford instead. The 'if d > dist[u] continue' check handles stale entries in lazy deletion.

algos.name: Union-Find (DSU)
algos.desc: Disjoint Set Union with path compression and union by rank. Efficiently handles connectivity queries and cycle detection in undirected graphs.
approach: Initialize parent[i] = i, rank[i] = 0
Find with path compression: parent[x] = find(parent[x])
Union by rank: attach smaller tree under larger
For cycle detection: if both nodes have same root → cycle
algos.tip: Use DSU for: Kruskal's MST, detecting cycles, number of connected components, dynamic connectivity.

algos.name: Graph Fundamentals
algos.desc: A structure consisting of nodes (vertices) and edges connected via an adjacency matrix or adjacency list.
approach: Represent graph globally HashMaps or List of Lists.
Navigate leveraging sets to track visited nodes to avoid infinite loops.
algos.tip: Always default to adjacency lists instead of adjacency matrices to save space (except for dense graphs where E is close to V²).

algos.name: Topological Sort
algos.desc: A linear ordering of graph vertices such that for every directed edge u -> v, vertex u comes before v. Used for dependency resolution.
approach: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS): Track in-degrees. Enqueue nodes with 0 in-degrees, decrement neighbors' in-degrees as you poll, and re-enqueue new 0 in-degree nodes.
DFS: Post-order DFS. Push nodes to a stack upon completion, then reverse the stack.
algos.tip: Almost always tested via prerequisite scheduling constraints in interviews (e.g., Course Schedule).

algos.name: Bellman-Ford
algos.desc: Computes shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted digraph. Able to handle negative edge weights.
approach: Initialize distances to infinity, source to 0.
Relax all edges V-1 times: if path via edge lowers distance, update.
Relax once more to detect negative weight cycles.
algos.tip: Crucial difference vs Dijkstra: it can find negative cycles. Only use if weights can be negative.

algos.name: Floyd-Warshall
algos.desc: An algorithm for finding shortest paths in a directed weighted graph with positive or negative edge weights between ALL pairs of vertices.
approach: Initialize a matrix distances to represent direct path weights.
Use a triple nested loop spanning intermediate node 'k', start computing path (i -> k) + (k -> j) < (i -> j)
algos.tip: Because it runs in O(V³), this algorithm guarantees test cases will have V <= 400. Be suspicious if n=100-300.

questions.name: Find the City With the Smallest Number of Neighbors at a Threshold Distance

algos.name: Kruskal's / Prim's MST
algos.desc: Algorithms that find a Minimum Spanning Tree for a connected weighted graph. Extracts paths linking all vertices with minimum total edge weight.
approach: Kruskal's: Sort edges by weight. Iterate and use Union-Find. If nodes aren't connected, include edge.
Prim's: Start at arbitrary node, maintain Priority Queue of edges that border visited set. Pick minimum valid edge until all visited.
algos.tip: Use Kruskal's with Union Find for sparse graphs, Prim's for dense graphs.

questions.name: Min Cost to Connect All Points

algos.name: 1D DP — Linear
algos.desc: Solves problems by building solution from subproblems stored in a 1D array. Classic: Fibonacci, climbing stairs, house robber.
approach: Define dp[i]: answer for subproblem of size i
Write recurrence: dp[i] = f(dp[i-1], dp[i-2], ...)
Initialize base cases
Iterate and fill; optimize space if only last k states needed
algos.tip: Always write the recurrence first, then the table, then optimize space. Draw a decision tree for clarity.

algos.name: 2D DP — Grid / String
algos.desc: dp[i][j] represents optimal solution using first i elements of one sequence and j of another. Used in LCS, LIS, edit distance, knapsack.
approach: Define state: dp[i][j] = answer considering i rows, j cols
Write recurrence for match and no-match cases
Fill row by row; first row/col = base cases
Trace back for actual sequence if needed
algos.tip: LCS → edit distance → coin change → knapsack are all 2D DP family. Space optimize with 1D rolling array when only previous row matters.

algos.name: Interval DP
algos.desc: Used when problem involves optimal answer over a subarray/interval [i,j]. State: dp[i][j]. Try all split points k in [i,j].
approach: Fill by increasing interval length
For each (i, j), try all split points k: i ≤ k < j
dp[i][j] = optimal over all splits
Base case: single elements dp[i][i]
algos.tip: Key: always fill by length of interval, not by starting index. Think of the last balloon burst, not the first.

algos.name: Bitmask DP
algos.desc: Uses a number's binary representation to represent dynamic states. Common in traveling salesman or assignments matching problems constraint exactly (1..N).
approach: A bit set to 1 means the item is visited/taken.
Use bitwise shifts to mark states.
Transition the dynamic programming grid tracking visited flags.
algos.tip: Any backtracking problem with N <= 20 is typically an optimal substructure target for Bitmask DP.

algos.name: Backtracking Template
algos.desc: Systematically explores all possibilities by building a solution incrementally and abandoning a path (backtrack) when it violates constraints.
approach: Choose: pick an element to add to current path
Explore: recurse with updated state
Unchoose: remove element (backtrack)
Prune: add conditions to skip invalid branches early
algos.tip: For permutations: swap in-place. For combinations/subsets: use start index. For duplicates: sort first and skip duplicates with 'i > start && nums[i] == nums[i-1]'.

algos.name: Greedy Strategy
algos.desc: Makes locally optimal choice at each step hoping to reach global optimum. Proof of correctness via exchange argument or matroid theory.
approach: Sort by the greedy criterion (finish time, ratio, etc.)
Make greedy choice at each step
Prove exchange argument: swapping any two elements doesn't improve solution
Verify greedy choice property and optimal substructure
algos.tip: Greedy fails when future choices affect current optimum (use DP instead). Classic greedy: sort by end time for interval scheduling.

algos.name: Divide & Conquer Fundamentals
algos.desc: An algorithm design paradigm that recursively breaks down a problem into two or more sub-problems of the same type, until they become simple enough to be solved directly.
approach: Divide the problem into non-overlapping subproblems.
Conquer the subproblems via recursive calls.
Combine the smaller results into a global composite answer.
algos.tip: Merge Sort and Binary Search are prime examples. Look for problems where splitting decisions doesn't cause recursive overlap logic.
